subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Exact component counts from provided rulebook text:
- 2 yellow ingredient books
- 2 green ingredient books
- 2 red ingredient books
- 2 blue ingredient books
- 2 purple ingredient books
- 1 orange ingredient book
- 1 black ingredient book
- 24 Fortune Teller cards
- 4 pots (player boards)
- 1 scoring track with turn indicator
- 215 ingredient chips + 3 replacement chips
- 4 bags
- 4 flasks
- 4 0/50 seal tiles
- 20 rubies
- 1 Almanac of Ingredients
- 1 flame (turn marker)
- 8 droplets (4 are for game variation)
- 1 bonus die
- 4 scoring markers
- 4 rat stones

Starting chips per player (taken from chip supply): 4 white 1-chips, 2 white 2-chips, 1 white 3-chip, 1 orange 1-chip, 1 green 1-chip.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1) Put the Scoring Track in the center.
2) Put the Flame on turn space 1 of the turn indicator.
3) Put the four 0/50 seals on their matching spaces with the “0” side up.
4) Lay out the Orange and Black ingredient books (always used).
5) Choose one set (1-4) for Green, Blue, Red, Yellow, Purple books. For first game use Set 1.
6) At setup start, place only Green, Blue, Red books from chosen set; keep Yellow and Purple aside (they enter in turns 2 and 3).
7) Sort ingredient chips by color and place near matching books.
8) Shuffle Fortune Teller cards and place face down near start player as draw pile; create discard next to scoring track when first revealed.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Each player takes one color set: 1 pot, 1 bag, 1 flask, 1 scoring marker, 1 rat stone.
- Place scoring marker on matching colored seal tile.
- Place pot in front of you with side without test tubes face up.
- Place droplet on “0” in center of pot.
- Place flask on large trivet, full side face up.
- Place rat stone on small trivet left of flask.
- Put the starting 9 chips into your bag: 4x white-1, 2x white-2, 1x white-3, 1x orange-1, 1x green-1.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
The last person who cooked something is start player and holds the Fortune Teller deck.

The game lasts 9 turns. At the start of each turn, reveal and read the top Fortune Teller card aloud. Resolve card timing by color:
- Purple Fortune Teller cards: resolve directly before turn begins.
- Blue Fortune Teller cards: effect lasts through turn or resolves at end as indicated.

Rulebook turn unlock reminder:
- Yellow ingredient book becomes active in turn 2.
- Purple ingredient book becomes active in turn 3.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each of the 9 turns follows this sequence:
1. Reveal and resolve Fortune Teller card.
2. Rats phase (from round 2 onward): all players behind leader count rat tails and move rat stone that many spaces ahead of droplet.
3. Potions phase (simultaneous): draw chips one at a time from bag and place in pot according to chip value; after each draw, choose stop or continue.
4. Explosion check: if total value of white chips in your pot exceeds 7, your pot explodes and you must stop drawing (last drawn chip still stays).
5. End of draw: once all players have stopped/exploded, begin evaluation.
6. Evaluation A-F in order (A bonus die, B end-phase chip effects, C rubies, D victory points, E buy chips with coins, F flask refill/droplet advancement as applicable).
7. Cleanup: return drawn chips to bag, advance turn marker, pass first-player Fortune Teller responsibility as needed by table flow.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Potions phase actions:
- Draw 1 chip blindly from your bag; you may never look into your bag.
- Place first chip immediately after droplet (or rat stone if used this turn).
- Place each later chip forward by its printed value (1/2/3/4) from the last placed chip; skipped spaces remain empty.
- After placing each chip, choose to stop safely or draw again.
- If bag is empty, you must stop.

Explosion rule:
- Add only white chip values.
- If white total exceeds 7, potion explodes immediately and you stop.
- Explosion does not cancel chip effects or Fortune Teller effects already applicable.

Chip effects timing:
- White and Orange chips: no extra powers.
- Blue, Red, Yellow: activate when drawn (immediate).
- Black, Green, Purple: evaluate in evaluation phase as specified by current ingredient books.
- You may decline optional chip effects.

Flask use:
- If the last chip you drew is white, you may use full flask to return that last white chip to your bag.
- Turn flask to empty side after use.
- You can continue drawing after using flask.
- Flask cannot be used if the draw caused explosion.
- Normally flask can be used once per turn and must later be refilled (evaluation step F).

Evaluation choices with/without explosion:
- Players whose pot did not explode take part in all standard scoring benefits, including bonus die contest.
- Players whose pot exploded do not roll bonus die and later must choose between taking VP (D) or buying chips with coins (E), not both.

Buying chips:
- Use coins shown by your scoring space to buy ingredient chips from available books/costs.
- Usual purchase limit is up to 2 chips, and they must be different colors (unless a special effect changes this).
- New chips go into your bag for future turns.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Rats catch-up system:
- Starting in turn 2, trailing players get temporary start-distance boosts by rat tails between them and leader on scoring track.
- Rat stone sets where your first chip placement can begin this turn; it does not permanently move your droplet.

Ingredient set system:
- Green/Blue/Red/Yellow/Purple each have 4 alternative rule sets (Set 1-4), dramatically changing strategy.
- Orange and Black books are always in game.

Bonus die system (Evaluation A):
- Among non-exploded players, whoever reached the highest scoring space rolls bonus die (all tied highest players roll).
- Bonus die can award VP, rubies, droplet advancement, or a free orange chip depending on roll face.

Scoring-space dual rewards:
- The space after your last placed chip gives both VP and coin value (subject to explosion restrictions on taking both).
- Ruby icons on reached spaces give rubies regardless of explosion status.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
End trigger:
- Game ends after the 9th turn’s evaluation and cleanup.

Final scoring includes all sources:
1) Victory points accumulated on the scoring track during all rounds.
2) Endgame conversion of remaining rubies into victory points (per rulebook conversion rate).
3) Endgame conversion of leftover money/coins into victory points (per rulebook conversion rate).
4) Any additional points granted by final-turn ingredient effects that award VP in your chosen ingredient set.

Winner:
- Player with highest total victory points wins.

Tiebreaker:
- If tied, tied player who reached farther scoring position by game end wins.
- If still tied, tied player with more rubies wins.
- If still tied, share victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Upgrade bag reliability before chasing huge turns.
Early buys should reduce white-chip risk density and increase useful low-cost color effects. A steady pot in turns 1-3 is better than one lucky spike.

2) Protect flask timing.
Treat flask as insurance against a bad white draw near your stop threshold. Don’t waste it on a harmless early draw unless your set effects strongly justify it.

3) Watch rat-tail economics.
If you trail slightly, rat stones can create efficient catch-up turns. Use that tempo to buy engine chips, not just to grab a one-time VP burst.

4) Buy chips that match your active set powers.
Set effects matter more than raw chip value. A cheap chip with strong set synergy often outperforms a pricier standalone chip.

5) Plan for turn-2/turn-3 unlocks.
Yellow and purple entering later changes value calculations. Keep enough coin flow to capitalize when those books open.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Over-pushing every round.
Repeated explosions force bad D/E choices and weaken long-term bag growth.

2) Ignoring white-chip probability.
Players often evaluate only the next draw, not remaining bag composition. Count your remaining white totals before deciding to continue.

3) Buying expensive chips too early.
High-cost chips can starve your bag of functional mid-cost pieces that stabilize turns and trigger synergies consistently.

4) Forgetting chip-timing windows.
Some effects trigger on draw, others at evaluation. Mis-timing leads to missed value and illegal sequencing.

5) Misusing flask after an explosion draw.
Rules explicitly prevent flask use if the last draw caused explosion. Track this carefully to avoid accidental take-backs.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Decision 1: Stop now vs draw one more
- Stopping secures guaranteed VP/coins and avoids explosion penalties.
- Drawing again can unlock stronger scoring space and effects but increases bust risk.

Decision 2: VP now vs economy now (especially after explosion)
- Taking VP keeps scoreboard pressure.
- Taking coins accelerates future bag quality and often yields better total points over 9 rounds.

Decision 3: Broad chip mix vs focused synergy
- Broad mix increases flexibility and reduces dependency on specific draws.
- Focused synergy can produce explosive turns if you see those chips together frequently.

metadata.notes: Primary content based on provided Quacks rulebook excerpt; ingredient-set specifics reference the Almanac/book system called out in the rules.
